openingMessage: Be more concerned with your character than your reputation, because your character is what you really are, while your reputation is merely what others think you are.
leadershipThought: Your awareness has to be higher than your intent. Intent doesn't matter if the impact is wrong. Operate in a diverse environment — different backgrounds, different beliefs, different life experiences.
opsContent:
Active Jobs (2026-07-01):
Robert Wright — SDU Tract complete, heading back (12:10 PM)
Michael Hutt — SM First Tee #121 well opened (3:47 PM)
Edward Carrillo — Nobles 3202D rescheduled, crane not on site; crew stood down and headed back (3:50 PM)
James Stephens — Diamondback Mabee 13-24 1-1H finished for the day (6:30 PM)
Stefanie Kirschke — Ovintiv Tom Ranch: cement shut-in, back at 7 AM Wednesday (6:20 PM)
Jacob Hernandez — Bizzel Irvin conversion complete, atmospheric testing confirmed safe, Co Rep released; then HP 616 (7:12–7:29 PM)
Caleb Smith — Shaw 13-8 #183 finished for the day (7:29 PM)
Ray Solis — University 3-21 complete; rolled to rig down at H&P 628 same evening (7:18 PM)

lessonsLearned.text: SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S confirmed (Dennis Easley, 3:51 PM). Multi-week elevated reading. Confirm monitoring plan and supplied air protocol before anyone dispatches to this location.
Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm and CO 3% on the 9 AM pull (Louie Zarate). Multi-gas hazard. Supplied air is not optional — confirm re-test and cascade plan at job start.
Nobles 3202D — crane not on site. Edward Carrillo made the call: crew stood down, job rescheduled. Confirm equipment is on location before you dispatch.

exxonMoments:
Stop Work Before the Air Cleared (WAME — Atmosphere Control)
Edward Carrillo stopped work at Turner E2 on June 27 after atmosphere readings showed benzene above exposure limits — even while the crew was already under air. He cleared the area, waited, ran a second test confirming safe conditions, then resumed. Every party on location was receptive when asked to stand down. That's not a slow-down — that's the plan working exactly as designed.

The Muster Point Was in the Wrong Place (WAME — Pre-Task Hazard Review)
Luis Zarate caught it during LMRA at Kendrick #5 on June 26: the main entry road — the default muster point — was downwind of the work zone. The crane crew had already set their exclusion zone before work started. Zarate redirected the muster point before anyone broke a connection. WAME isn't a form you fill out — it's noticing what's wrong before it becomes a problem.

Casing Leak Stopped the Job Cold (WAME — SWA and Area Control)
During hookup at Glass L 3 on June 26, Stefanie Kirschke and Edward Carrillo found a failed nipple on the casing — a leak that wasn't in the plan. They called it, secured the area, bled down the well with vac and pump truck, and built a mitigation plan before any work continued. The location was left safe and controlled. The learn: thorough pre-job wellhead inspection belongs in the scope, not the afterthought.

LSRA, Hold Points, EAP — All of It, Before the First Bolt Moved (WAME — Job Hazard Analysis)
Baldemar Bracamontes at Natalie 26 on June 26: crew ran through every LSRA tied to the job scope, broke the work into steps with a hold point before each new phase, identified possible triggers, and had an EAP staged with both Canon and roustabout crews briefed in English and Spanish. The job finished without using the cascade system or the EAP. That's the point — you build the plan so you don't need it under pressure.

fieldNews.headline: Ray Solis finished at the University and rolled straight to a rig down at H&P 628 the same evening — no gap, no waiting, just moved.
Stefanie Kirschke worked through a cement shut-in delay at Ovintiv Tom Ranch — posted updates all day, confirmed the schedule, and had a clean 7 AM return locked in before she left.
James Stephens put Diamondback Mabee 13-24 1-1H to bed and posted up in Breakroom before heading out. Short message, job done.
Michael Hutt got SM First Tee #121 well opened and back in play — posted the update so dispatch and the team know the location is active.
Robert Wright wrapped the SDU Tract job clean and headed back. Job complete, crew moving — that's all you need to post.
SM Energy Gipple 154H — 2,000ppm H2S. Dennis Easley flagged it Tuesday afternoon. This location is not a walk-up — confirm your monitoring plan before you leave the yard.
Cosmo phase 2 — H2S 3,000ppm and CO 3% on the 9 AM pull. Louie Zarate confirmed both readings. Supplied air is not optional at this location — it is the plan.
Nobles 3202D — crane was not on site. Edward Carrillo made the call: crew stood down, job rescheduled. Confirm your equipment is on location before you dispatch.

todaysChallenge: On your next job, walk the work area before a single bolt moves. Name the exclusion zones, the muster point, and the atmospheric plan out loud.
todaysChallengeSubtext: WAME is a conversation, not a checkbox. If you can't answer where the hazards are and what you'd do if one activates, you're not ready to start.
